The system builds a rigorous staged validation and metric tracking framework. The core of model debugging focuses on the generalization evaluation of regression and classification: Multi-dimensional Regression Metrics: The model evaluates not only traditional average errors, but the most critical core metric is the multi-output variance-weighted coefficient of determination. To dissect 2D kinematic decoding more delicately, the code additionally splits and outputs the goodness of fit for independent coordinate axes. Classification Accuracy Monitoring: The evaluation standard focuses on strict classification accuracy. For data with multiple discrete categories, an increase in accuracy directly reflects the effective overlap of the feature manifolds. Periodic Validation & Checkpointing: The adversarial training process is highly unstable; therefore, after iterating a specific number of rounds, the system places the generator in validation mode and inputs the validation set. The system tracks the performance of the validation set after it is aligned and fed into the frozen source domain decoder. The generator and decoder weights corresponding to peak performance are saved as independent files. End-to-End Logging & Post-Processing: The framework features built-in logging objects and summarizes daily validation results into specific spreadsheet files. To adapt to further data analysis pipelines, users can also utilize scripts to losslessly map the data matrices (with headers removed) back into tensor files.

Relying on unpaired domain adaptation capabilities, this Cycle-GAN architecture precisely resolves the most acute pain points in the current field of long-term implanted BCIs: Zero-Retraining Reuse Constrained by microscopic changes in the physical environment of the brain, even the most advanced decoders encounter a performance cliff a few days after model calibration. This system ingeniously shifts the paradigm: instead of modifying the massive downstream decoder, it corrects the input data itself. By using the aligner to pull future shifted features back into the feature space of the initial training day, it vastly extends the lifespan of a single decoder. Overcoming the Label Scarcity Dilemma for Long-term Offline Data Cycle-GAN is essentially an unsupervised generative adversarial architecture (the calculation of core losses requires no involvement of true labels). This implies that, in real-world scenarios, patients do not need to spend hours each day repeating annotated calibration paradigms. The system relies solely on a small amount of unlabeled EEG sequences freely emitted by the patient to silently complete manifold space reconstruction and adaptive alignment in the background. Generalized Adaptation to Highly Heterogeneous Neural Tasks Whether performing continuous 2D force trajectory regression on macaque datasets or executing cross-week multi-intention pattern classification, this alignment pipeline demonstrates cross-paradigm universality. By simply replacing the trailing regressor or classifier, a unified feature manifold aligner can seamlessly drive a variety of diverse and heterogeneous downstream tasks. Offline Domain Adaptation & Online Decoupled Execution Cycle-GAN occupies a highly unique ecological niche within the system. Its highly time-consuming generative adversarial training and feature manifold alignment processes are typically strictly confined to execution on offline computing clusters or during cross-day pre-session calibration phases. However, because the architecture achieves perfect physical decoupling between the "alignment generator" and the "downstream decoder" by design, once the generator finalizes the weight solidification for cross-day manifold mapping, the data stream calibrated by real-time mapping can be fed into the downstream network with zero latency. This provides a pure, highly robust online real-time closed-loop operating environment for complex control tasks.

This project provides a way to preprocess the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset by transforming raw EEG signals into signals for model training. Only two subsets of the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset were used in the project. The first subset contains only performed left or right hand motor tasks. The second subset contains only imagined left or right hand motor tasks. In both subsets, a total of 103 out of 109 subjects were used, and subjects 38, 88, 89, 92, 100, and 104 were omitted due to data annotation errors.

The BCI2000 system was used to record 64-channel EEG signals. The physical electrode layout strictly follows the international 10-10 system (excluding peripheral or specific electrodes such as Nz, F9, F10, FT9, FT10, A1, A2, TP9, TP10, P9, and P10).

The experiment primarily focuses on Motor Execution and Motor Imagery. Participants are required to respond according to target cues displayed on the screen. Each participant must complete 14 experimental runs, which include: Baseline state: 2 runs of 1 minute each (eyes closed resting, eyes open resting). Task state: 12 runs of 2 minutes each (the following 4 tasks are repeated 3 times): Task 1: Execute opening and closing of the left or right fist based on left-right cues displayed on the screen. Task 2: Imagine opening and closing the left or right fist based on left-right cues displayed on the screen. Task 3: Execute opening and closing of both fists (up) or both feet (down) based on up-down cues displayed on the screen. Task 4: Imagine opening and closing both fists (up) or both feet (down) based on up-down cues displayed on the screen.
160 Hz (i.e., 160 data samples are collected per second per channel).

Original Data Format: The data is provided in EDF+ format (containing 64 EEG signal channels and 1 event annotation channel), accompanied by an .event annotation file for parsing. Preprocessed Format: After filtering and segmenting, the data is outputted in the commonly used NumPy array format for machine learning, and saved in a compressed .npz file (e.g., EMM_task1_preprocessed.npz) for easy direct use in code.

This pipeline is designed to convert raw EEG recording data into a standardized tensor format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from data loading to feature and label construction. 1. Data Loading The core function load_data() is used to directly read and load the 64-channel raw EEG experimental data from 109 volunteers, recorded by the BCI2000 system, from the local folder. 2. Signal Preprocessing & Chunking A notch filter is applied to the EEG signal to eliminate 60 Hz power line noise, followed by band-pass filtering between 2 Hz and 60 Hz. The experimental data is then strictly truncated into 4-second segments (at 160 Hz sampling rate, resulting in 640 samples). Using a sliding window method, the data is further divided into 8 non-overlapping time segments (each window contains 80 samples). 3. Feature Extraction Features are extracted from the filtered and chunked data, and formatted into a NumPy array that can be processed by both CPU and GPU. A feature matrix with the shape (number of samples, number of channels, number of time points) is constructed and saved in .npz format to the output folder. 4. Label Generation & Mapping Each segmented window is precisely mapped to its corresponding experimental condition, receiving the same target labels as the original trials (e.g., representing the type of movement and whether the movement is for the left or right hand). The final supervised label array is generated and aligned with the shape (number of samples, labels).

PhysioBank, PhysioToolkit, and PhysioNet: Components of a new research resource for complex physiologic signals. Circulation [Online]. 101 (23), pp. e215–e220. RRID:SCR_007345.

The Language Transformer is an end-to-end deep architecture specifically constructed for the analysis of high-frequency neural electrophysiological signals and continuous Neural Speech Decoding. In the field of Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI), particularly when confronting complex sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) mapping tasks, traditional recurrent neural networks (e.g., LSTMs) are frequently constrained by the serial computational bottleneck of time steps and gradient vanishing over exceedingly long sequences. This architecture thoroughly abandons the temporal recurrent paradigm under the Markov assumption, introducing a pure Global Multi-Head Self-Attention mechanism. Combined with the 11_Speech-style Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) inference framework, the model is capable of instantaneously capturing non-linear long-range dependencies between any two time steps across the entire neural sequence when processing a single speech trial (Single-trial), achieving parallel decoding from high-dimensional spatio-temporal dynamic manifolds to discrete semantic symbols. From the perspectives of theoretical mechanisms and network topology, this architecture consists of the following core modules: Frontend Filtration & Manifold Unfolding Neural population activities are often accompanied by high-frequency transient Poisson noise. Rather than simply passing the signal through, the underlying SpeechFeatureFrontend of the model executes a rigorous set of physical preprocessing steps: High-frequency Noise Filtration: Utilizes GaussianSmoothing to perform one-dimensional convolutional smoothing, filtering out high-frequency artifacts in the time domain. Cross-day Domain Shift Compensation: Incorporates built-in day_weights and day_bias parameter matrices to execute linear calibration of the cross-day feature space directly at the input end via affine transformations. Local Receptive Field Reconstruction: Following Softsign activation, it employs nn.Unfold to segment the one-dimensional temporal signal into overlapping two-dimensional windowed features. This operation significantly mitigates the inherent shortcoming of the Transformer in capturing local features. High-dimensional Projection & Positional Encoding Because Transformers inherently lack the Inductive Bias to process sequence order, the features output by the frontend are first aligned to a unified hidden manifold dimensionality (d_model) via linear projection. Subsequently, the model introduces Positional Encoding based on sine and cosine functions, seamlessly injecting absolute temporal rhythm information into the neural features, endowing the model with the ability to parse the temporal evolution laws of spike sequences. Deep Global Attention Encoder The core computational engine of the model is constructed by stacking multiple TransformerEncoderLayers. Multi-Head Attention: Allows the model to simultaneously attend to the temporal dependencies of multiple physiological dimensions, such as articulatory preparation, muscle activation, and acoustic feedback, within different feature subspaces. Norm-First Architecture: Explicitly sets norm_first=True. In exceedingly deep topological structures, this design has been proven to substantially enhance the backpropagation efficiency of gradients within Residual Connections, guaranteeing absolute training stability. Asynchronous CTC Decoding After the model output is mapped to the vocabulary space, nn.CTCLoss is introduced. Through probability Marginalization, this mechanism allows for end-to-end training between unaligned input spike sequences and target text sequences, thoroughly breaking the reliance of traditional decoders on forced frame-level alignment.

The training stability of the Language Transformer is exceptionally dependent on the purity of the underlying data stream. The framework establishes exceedingly rigorous engineering specifications for the ingestion of temporal neural tensors: Rigorous Neural Temporal Tensorization: The underlying engine strictly mandates that the feature matrix X be a standard three-dimensional tensor (N, T, C) (i.e., trials, time steps, channels), while the semantic label y be a two-dimensional sequence (N, S). Dynamic Boundary Alignment: Given the variable lengths of speech intentions, the framework requires all samples to be accompanied by true physical boundary records (X_len and y_len). The Transformer's Attention Mask will dynamically truncate strictly according to these boundaries, completely eliminating the contamination of attention weights by invalid Padding Tokens. Unidirectional Statistic Freezing: The system adopts an absolutely isolated anti-leakage strategy when fitting the feature distribution (Z-score statistics). The mean and standard deviation are independently fitted solely within the training domain and mapped unidirectionally to the validation and test domains in a frozen state, ensuring the absolute authenticity of generalization evaluations. Stochastic Perturbation Injection: During training forward propagation, the framework supports the dynamic injection of Gaussian white noise (white_noise_sd) and channel-level constant offset noise (constant_offset_sd), forcing the global attention mechanism to learn invariant feature representations that are more robust against electrode impedance variations.

The following tables meticulously compile the parameter space utilized for Transformer training and tuning within the cer.py script. To guarantee the precision of academic reproducibility, the core override rules (Overrides) for two distinct distribution shift scenarios—single-day decoding and cross-day decoding—have been exhaustively integrated.

Parameter Name Type Default/Baseline Value Theoretical Explanation & Scenario Override Rules (Overrides) input_dim int - The input channel/electrode dimensionality of the raw neural interface. vocab_size int - The vocabulary space size for discrete semantic symbols (strictly inclusive of the CTC blank token). d_model int 256 The non-linear representation bandwidth of the Transformer's core hidden state. Fixed at 256 for both single-day and cross-day decoding scenarios. n_heads int 8 The number of heads in the multi-head attention mechanism. Maintained at 8 across all scenarios to capture multi-dimensional physiological correlations. num_layers int 4 The number of stacked encoder layers determining the depth of temporal abstraction. Maintained at 4 across all scenarios. dropout float 0.2 The neuron dropout rate in the attention weight matrices and feed-forward layers, used to block over-fitting co-adaptation. kernel_len int 32 The core parameter determining the receptive field of the frontend feature unfolding. Core Strategy: In single-day and cross-day decoding scenarios, it is uniformly overridden and tightened to 14 to compress redundant information. stride_len int 4 The stride of the local receptive field sliding over time steps. gaussian_smooth_width float 2.0 The standard deviation (Sigma) controlling the intensity of Gaussian low-pass filtering. use_day_idx / --no_day_idx bool True Core Shift Calibration: Whether to activate the frontend cross-day weight calibration. Forcibly disabled (False) in cross-day decoding scenarios, compelling the attention network itself to learn domain-agnostic feature representations; remains enabled (True) for single-day decoding.

Parameter Name Type Default/Baseline Value Theoretical Explanation & Scenario Override Rules (Overrides) batch_size int 32 The sample batch size for gradient estimation. Uniformly set to 32 across all scenarios. epochs int 50 The global iteration upper bound for empirical risk minimization. Uniformly set to 50 across all scenarios. lr float 0.0003 The initial step size driving the AdamW parameter space exploration. weight_decay float 0.0001 The L2 penalty term used to suppress the weight matrix norm, preventing the attention network from forcibly memorizing noise patterns. patience int 10 Generalization boundary defense: the tolerance period for early stopping and annealing scheduling. white_noise_sd float 0.2 The intensity of injected Gaussian white noise, simulating electrode thermal noise. constant_offset_sd float 0.05 The injected channel-level DC offset noise, simulating microelectrode baseline drift. val_ratio float 0.2 The split ratio for establishing a generalization evaluation surrogate dataset based on random sampling. seed int 42 The underlying random number generator anchor, ensuring absolute reproducibility of attention matrix initialization.

In the domain of continuous language decoding, model evaluation must transcend simple frame-level accuracy and focus on the fidelity of the global sequence topology: Levenshtein Sequence Evaluation: The framework discards the rigid cross-entropy metric, adopting the Character Error Rate (CER) as the sole guiding north star. By calculating the minimum number of insertions, deletions, and substitutions (i.e., Levenshtein distance) between the model's greedily decoded sequence and the true semantic symbols, CER can most intuitively reflect the usability of the BCI system in real-world communication scenarios. Mandatory Dynamical Stabilization: Self-attention mechanisms are prone to gradient distribution anomalies in deep networks. To circumvent such risks, the underlying optimization engine is hard-coded to execute strict clip_grad_norm_ (threshold 1.0) clipping between every forward/backward step. Complemented by the Norm-First architecture, this serves as a dual physical safety perimeter for model training. Adaptive Annealing & Generalization Defense: The system deeply integrates the ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler. When the model is trapped in a local saddle point on the error rate surface, the scheduler automatically performs learning rate halving annealing (factor=0.5). Coupled with rigorous validation set state monitoring and an optimal weight snapshot (best_model.pt) rollback mechanism, it perfectly intercepts the collapse of generalization capability caused by overfitting to noisy data.

Leveraging its global attention mechanism, the Language Transformer architecture demonstrates revolutionary application potential in the field of temporal neural decoding: Alignment-Free Natural Language Generation Traditional decoding systems require data to possess precise "frame-to-phoneme" level labels, whereas this model utilizes the marginalization capability of CTC and the global receptive field of the Transformer to allow the direct use of full-sentence text as target labels. This characteristic drastically reduces the cost of neural data annotation, making real-time speech BCIs that "directly generate natural language from brain intentions" a reality. Global Traceability of High-Fidelity Features Compared to the implicit memory flow of LSTMs, the Multi-Head Attention Weights of the Transformer possess extremely high interpretability. It can automatically filter out transient noise in neural population firing (by assigning extremely low weights) and focus high-weight attention on firing clusters with genuine semantic relevance over extremely long time spans, achieving precise mapping from high-frequency electrophysiological signals to text. Deep Robustness against Non-stationary Domain Shifts The greatest nightmare facing brain-computer interface systems is the long-term drift of signal distributions. Through flexible parameter tuning (such as explicitly disabling use_day_idx and adjusting kernel_len in cross-day scenarios), researchers can utilize the exceptionally strong global abstraction capacity of the Transformer to force the network to learn genuine "neural semantic invariants," thereby achieving the stable maintenance of cross-day long-term decoding performance.

LFADS (Latent Factor Analysis via Dynamical Systems) is a Variational Sequential Autoencoder specifically designed for neuroscience data. In the fields of Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI) and computational neuroscience, single-trial neural spike data is often accompanied by extremely high Poisson noise. LFADS breaks through the limitations of traditional Gaussian smoothing or trial-averaging methods. Its core hypothesis is that complex neural population activity is driven by a low-dimensional, non-linear intrinsic dynamical system. From the perspective of theoretical mechanisms, the core architecture of LFADS consists of the following five key components: Variational Sequential Autoencoder LFADS deeply integrates the powerful temporal modeling capabilities of Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) with the probabilistic framework of Variational Inference. Instead of outputting a single deterministic trajectory, it learns the posterior probability distribution of latent variables, thereby achieving unprecedented robustness and generalization capabilities in highly noisy single-spike data. Initial Condition Inference At the onset of every motor or cognitive task, the brain's dynamical system resides in a specific initial state. LFADS utilizes a backward-running RNN encoder (IC Encoder) to read the historical information of the entire neural sequence, accurately inferring the initial condition that drives the dynamical evolution of the current trial, along with its distribution probability. Autonomous Dynamical Generator Serving as the core state transition module of LFADS, the generator (typically a multi-layer RNN) receives the initial conditions and autonomously evolves forward in time based on its learned non-linear dynamical rules. This process effectively characterizes the intrinsic low-dimensional manifold properties exhibited by the motor cortex during action execution. Inferred Inputs & Controller To address unpredictable external stimuli or feedback inputs from unrecorded brain regions during brain activity, LFADS innovatively introduces a Controller Input Encoder (CI Encoder) and an RNN Controller. These two modules dynamically infer external perturbations at each time step and inject them as time-varying inputs into the generator in real-time. This significantly enhances the model's ability to fit non-autonomous, sudden neural activities. Poisson Emission & Firing Rate Reconstruction The final output of LFADS does not directly fit discrete spike sequences. Instead, it maps the high-dimensional states of the generator to low-dimensional Latent Factors, and subsequently uses exponential mapping (to ensure non-negativity) to reconstruct the underlying true and smooth neuron Firing Rates. The model employs Poisson Negative Log-Likelihood as its reconstruction target, strictly aligning with the statistical physics laws of neural spike emission.

As an unsupervised generative model, the preprocessing of LFADS input data must strictly adhere to neurophysical standards: Spike Binning & Tensorization: The input data must consist of discrete Spike Counts, rather than smoothed continuous signals. The time series of neuron firings are typically divided into fixed time windows, constructing a three-dimensional tensor containing trials, time steps, and neuron channels. Coordinated Dropout: This is the core feature engineering mechanism that guarantees LFADS's generalization ability. During the training phase, the model randomly masks the spike data of certain neurons in the input tensor, forcing the generator to rely solely on the Population Dynamics of the remaining neurons to infer the firing rates of the masked ones. This effectively prevents the autoencoder from degrading into a meaningless Identity Mapping. Trial Alignment: To ensure that "Initial Condition Inference" can effectively capture cross-trial variability, continuous EEG or spike signals usually need to be strictly aligned and segmented on the time scale based on specific behavioral events (e.g., target onset, visual stimulus presentation).

This document provides a comprehensive compilation of the core parameters utilized in training, testing, and hyperparameter optimization scripts based on the LFADS (Latent Factor Analysis via Dynamical Systems) architecture. It specifically targets classification and regression tasks for sequential data, such as electroencephalography (EEG) and neural spike trains. Parameters are organized into four logical modules and application scenarios, covering everything from top-level interface calls to the internal configurations of the LFADS core network.

These parameters are exposed externally to facilitate rapid data-driven feature extraction and evaluation. They support both Python API invocations and Command Line Interface (CLI) arguments. The values integrated below include default settings, single-day/cross-day decoding baselines, and specific Override rules for distinct datasets. Parameter / CLI Flag Type Task Default / Single-Day Baseline Detailed Description & Cross-Day Dataset Overrides train_datapath str Universal None Path to the training data file. Automatically split if no test set is provided. val_datapath str Universal None Path to the validation data file. test_datapath str Universal None Path to the test data file for evaluation. fac_dim int Universal 40 Core Latent Factor Dimension. Default and cross-day baseline are 40. Note: Overridden to 24 for 10_VisualGratingTask and 12_Music cross-day classification; remains 40 for Jango_force. batch_size int Universal 64 Batch size for data loading. Note: Reduced to 32 for 10_VisualGratingTask and 12_Music cross-day classification; remains 64 for Jango_force. epochs int Universal 50 Total training epochs. Note: Overridden to 60 for 10_VisualGratingTask/12_Music and 80 for Jango_force in cross-day classification. lr float Universal 0.004 Initial learning rate. Note: Reduced to 0.0005 for 10_VisualGratingTask/12_Music and 0.003 for Jango_force in cross-day scenarios. dropout float Universal 0.02 Dropout rate. Note: Increased to 0.1 for 10_VisualGratingTask/12_Music cross-day classification; remains 0.02 for Jango_force. patience int Universal 10 Early stopping patience. Note: Uniformly overridden to 15 for cross-day classification on 10_VisualGratingTask, 12_Music, and Jango_force. weight_decay float Universal 0 L2 regularization coefficient. Disabled by default. Note: Overridden to 0.0001 for 10_VisualGratingTask and 12_Music cross-day classification. reduce str Regress. "center" Aggregation method for target sequences: extracts the center frame or computes the mean. val_ratio float Universal 0.2 Ratio used to split training data when validation/test sets are not independently provided. Maintained at 0.2 across all scenarios. seed int Universal 42 Global random seed to ensure experimental reproducibility. device str Universal "auto" Automated detection of computing hardware (CPU/GPU). output_dir str Universal "./outputs" Parent directory for model checkpoints, output files, and logs. run_name str Universal Auto-gen. Unique folder name for a single execution, typically generated via timestamp.

documents.t: This section details the internal architectural configurations of the LFADS model, which govern the generative and inference logic of the RNN-based autoencoder. These are typically passed via configuration files or dictionary objects. Parameter Name Type Detailed Description encod_data_dim / encod_seq_len int Encoder Input Config: Number of input data channels and the sequence length (time steps). recon_seq_len int Decoder Output Config: Sequence length for the reconstructed target data. ic_enc_seq_len / ic_enc_dim int Sequence length and RNN dimensionality for the Initial Condition (IC) encoder. ci_enc_dim / ci_lag int Dimensionality and temporal lag for the Controller Input encoder. con_dim / co_dim int Dimensionality of the RNN controller and its outputs. ic_dim / gen_dim / fac_dim int Latent Variable Dimensions: Respectively govern the initial condition dimension, RNN generator dimension, and output latent factor dimension. variational bool Enables variational inference. If False, the model reverts to a deterministic autoencoder. ic_post_var_min float Sets the lower bound for the IC posterior variance to prevent variance collapse. l2_start_epoch / l2_increase_epoch int The epoch at which L2 regularization begins, and the duration of its linear ramp-up to the maximum value. l2_ic_enc_scale / l2_gen_scale (etc.) float L2 regularization scaling factors specifically for different network modules. kl_start_epoch / kl_increase_epoch int The epoch at which KL divergence constraints begin, and the duration of its linear ramp-up (annealing). kl_ic_scale / kl_co_scale float KL divergence penalty weights for the IC and controller output posterior distributions.

documents.t: In neuroscience, LFADS performance evaluation cannot rely solely on traditional Mean Squared Error or cross-entropy. It must be tightly integrated with its Poisson emission characteristics: Bits Per Spike (BPS): This is the golden standard for evaluating the performance of generative models on spike train processing. Based on the Poisson Negative Log-Likelihood, BPS measures the additional information gain provided by the instantaneous firing rates reconstructed by LFADS compared to a baseline model. A higher BPS value indicates a richer capture of neural dynamical details. PSTH Reconstruction Fidelity: When validating the model's denoising effectiveness, the single-trial firing rates inferred by the model are typically averaged and used to calculate the Coefficient of Determination against the empirical Peri-Stimulus Time Histogram (PSTH). An excellent LFADS model can restore the PSTH with high fidelity while maintaining single-trial variability. Posterior Variance Monitoring: During the model debugging process, the posterior variance of the initial conditions and the controller outputs must be closely monitored. If the variance collapses to an artificially set lower bound, it indicates that the model has failed to effectively utilize the variational mechanism to learn data uncertainty. In this case, the KL annealing strategy must be adjusted promptly.

Relying on the five core mechanisms mentioned above, LFADS has successfully broken through several intractable theoretical and engineering challenges in the modern BCI domain: Achieving High-Fidelity "Single-trial" Closed-Loop Control Real-world BCIs must respond instantly based on single-trial brain intentions. Utilizing the synergy between the "Autonomous Dynamical Generator" and the "Controller", LFADS accurately extracts smooth firing rates from highly noisy single spikes without relying on historical trial averaging. This drastically improves the Signal-to-Noise Ratio of real-time intention decoding. Manifold Alignment & Stabilizing BCIs Neural signals recorded by implanted microelectrode arrays suffer from severe non-stationary drift over time (e.g., electrode micromotion, glial cell encapsulation). Given that the "Latent Factors" extracted by LFADS truly represent the inherent low-dimensional dynamical manifold of neural populations, researchers can align the latent factor spaces across days. This ensures that downstream BCI decoders maintain extremely high and stable performance over months, completely eliminating the cost of frequent and expensive model recalibration. Decoding Unrecorded Brain Region Interference During operation, the motor cortex is often interfered with by visual feedback, tactile feedback, or spontaneous activity from other brain regions. LFADS's "Inferred Inputs & Controller" mechanism not only effectively improves signal reconstruction accuracy but also provides neuroscientists with a method to reverse-analyze and infer these unobserved external input signals. This serves as an exceptionally valuable analytical tool for exploring whole-brain network connectivity. Offline Dynamics Tracing & Delayed Block Decoding From a computational paradigm perspective, LFADS's "Initial Condition Inference" strongly relies on the bidirectional recurrent neural network reading the entire historical and future sequence to achieve high-fidelity neural manifold smoothing. This non-causal characteristic dictates that its current core battlefield lies in Offline single-trial neural dynamics analysis and scientific discovery. If it needs to be deployed in a real-time BCI closed-loop system, engineering compromises must be made by adopting a Block Decoding strategy with a specific computational delay to find the optimal balance between smoothing fidelity and control latency.

Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) is a specialized Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture, originally proposed by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber in 1997. Its primary objective is to resolve the vanishing and exploding gradient problems encountered by traditional RNNs when processing long sequential data. By introducing three gating mechanisms (the input gate, forget gate, and output gate), LSTM allows the network to selectively retain or discard information during the learning process, thereby demonstrating superior performance in tasks involving long-term dependencies. From the perspectives of code implementation and network topology, this LSTM architecture comprises the following core modules: Sequential Feature Encoder The raw input typically consists of high-dimensional, multi-channel neural or physiological signals. The foundational layer of the model relies on a highly encapsulated RNNEncoder component for sequence ingestion. By configuring the number of network layers and the bidirectional flag, the model constructs a deep, bidirectional receptive field, simultaneously capturing historical context and future evolutionary trends at the current time step. Gating Mechanism & State Transition Unlike the Transformer, which relies on explicit positional encoding, the LSTM intrinsically aligns with tasks exhibiting strong temporal dependencies due to its recursive topology. Through the precise coordination of the forget gate, input gate, and output gate, the network can autonomously determine the "retention" or "forgetting" of neural potential features over extended time-step propagation. This core gating mechanism effectively overcomes the gradient vanishing bottleneck inherent in traditional RNNs. Dual-Track Task Head To seamlessly adapt to multi-scenario paradigms such as BCI, the model incorporates a highly flexible branched decoding logic at the output end: Pooled Classification Decoding: For discrete motor intention recognition or qualitative analysis of entire signal segments, the model provides flexible pooling strategies. Whether applying global average pooling across the temporal dimension or precisely extracting the state at the final step of the sequence, the features subsequently flow into cascaded fully connected layers equipped with Layer Normalization and Dropout, outputting the final classification logits. Time-Distributed Regression Decoding: When addressing Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) regression requirements, such as continuous kinematic coordinate resolution, the model employs a dedicated TimeDistributedLinear module. Bypassing temporal pooling, this module applies a linear projection with Layer Normalization directly across each time step of the sequence length independently, utilizing shared weights. This facilitates frame-by-frame, high-fidelity tracking of high-frequency dynamic systems.

To ensure the training stability of the model on complex sequential data (such as EEG signals and time-series sensor signals), the framework establishes strict standardized protocols for data engineering: Tensor Dimensionality Constraints: All input feature matrices must be strictly cast into standard three-dimensional array formats, representing the number of trials/samples, time steps, and channels/features, respectively. Target Label Alignment: Classification Tasks: The labels are required to be one-dimensional arrays. The framework incorporates comprehensive methods to automatically and accurately map labels—whether in the form of text, bytes, or non-contiguous discrete numerical values—into contiguous integer indices starting from 0. Regression Tasks: It is strictly mandated that the label matrix assumes a three-dimensional shape, and the time steps of the features must be perfectly aligned with the time steps of the labels. Feature Normalization and Strict Data Isolation (Z-score Normalization & Data Isolation): To eliminate the adverse effects of feature dimensional discrepancies on gradient updates, the framework adopts rigorous engineering practices to eradicate data leakage. The system independently fits the statistical metrics exclusively on the training set, and unidirectionally, statically maps the generated normalization statistics to the validation and test sets. This ensures absolute fairness in model performance evaluation and reflects its true generalization capability. Feature Selection and Engineering: For complex time-series data, the performance of the LSTM model can be further enhanced through feature selection, PCA dimensionality reduction, or feature engineering in both the temporal and frequency domains.

This document provides a comprehensive detailing of the parameters utilized in the training, evaluation, and visualization scripts for sequence classification and regression tasks employing LSTM/GRU/RNN architectures. Parameters are categorized by module and application scenario, encompassing batch execution configurations, core training and evaluation hyperparameters, and plotting utility settings. To ensure exhaustiveness, specific parameter overrides for single-day decoding, cross-day decoding, and the Jango_force dataset have been wholly integrated into the "Detailed Description" column, ensuring no data omissions.

This file is primarily designed for batch traversing datasets within directories to train and evaluate various RNN variants (LSTM/GRU/RNN). The global configuration parameters located at the top of the script are described below: Parameter Name Type Default / Example Detailed Description ROOT_DIR str '/data/.../data' Root directory path storing the datasets; supports nested subdirectories. FILE_GLOB str "*.npz" Wildcard string for matching data files; supports recursive searching. RECURSIVE bool True Boolean flag indicating whether to recursively search all subdirectories within ROOT_DIR. TASK str "cls" Task objective: "cls" denotes a classification task, whereas "reg" denotes a regression task. RNN_TYPES_TO_TRY list ["lstm", "gru", "rnn"] List of RNN architectural variants to evaluate sequentially (bar charts will be rendered in this specified order). HIDDEN_SIZE int 64 Feature dimension size of the RNN hidden states. NUM_LAYERS int 3 Number of stacked RNN layers. BIDIRECTIONAL bool True Boolean flag indicating the use of a Bidirectional RNN. DROPOUT float 0.3 Dropout probability. POOLING str "mean" Sequence pooling mechanism (exclusive to classification tasks): "mean" (average across time steps) or "last" (extracts the final time step). LR float 1e-3 Learning rate for the optimizer (Adam). WEIGHT_DECAY float 0.0 L2 regularization penalty coefficient. BATCH_SIZE int 128 Training batch size. MAX_EPOCHS int 30 Maximum number of training epochs. PATIENCE int 100 Patience threshold for Early Stopping (i.e., training halts if the validation metric fails to improve for this number of consecutive epochs). VAL_RATIO float 0.2 Proportion of the dataset allocated for validation. For classification tasks, stratified sampling is automatically applied to maintain the class distribution. SEED int 31 Random seed to ensure experimental reproducibility. PREDICTION_MODE str "seq" Prediction mode for regression tasks: "seq" entails step-by-step trajectory prediction, whereas "final" outputs only the terminal state result. ZSCORE bool True Boolean flag indicating whether to apply Z-score standardization to the input features. DEVICE str "cuda_if_available" Hardware acceleration configuration for training.

Note: Jango_force cross-day maintains 0.0001. seed int Universal 42 Global random seed, guaranteeing full experimental reproducibility. device str Universal "auto" Computational device selection. num_layers int Universal 2 Number of LSTM layers. Note: The Jango_force cross-day classification override is 3. hidden_size int Universal Class.: 64 / Regress.: 128 LSTM feature dimension. Fixed at 64 for both single-day and cross-day decoding classifications. Note: Jango_force cross-day classification utilizes 64. dropout float Universal 0.1 Dropout probability. Cross-day decoding elevates this to 0.2. Note: Jango_force cross-day classification further elevates this to 0.3. --bidirectional / --no_bidirectional flag Universal False (Architecture) /
True (Training Flag) Parameter Logic Explanation: Bidirectional processing is enabled by default (True). The underlying network architecture initializes as unidirectional, but the training script resets it to bidirectional by default. To execute a unidirectional model in strictly causal scenarios (e.g., online closed-loop systems), explicitly pass the --no_bidirectional flag. pooling str Classification Only "mean" Sequence feature aggregation method. Uniformly utilizes "mean" across default, single-day, and cross-day scenarios. Note: The Jango_force cross-day classification override is "last". output_dir str Universal "./outputs" Base directory for outputting model weights, logs, and evaluation results. run_name str Universal Timestamp Sub-directory name for a single execution; auto-generated via timestamp by default. log_csv str Universal Auto-generated File path for saving training loss and metric trajectory logs. --save_ckpt flag Universal True Flag to save the optimal model weights (checkpoints) during the training phase. --plot flag Universal False Flag to automatically generate learning curve plots upon training completion.

documents.t: This script is utilized for parsing log files generated during training and outputting line charts depicting metric trends. Debugging and evaluating the LSTM model typically involves the following aspects: Evaluation Metrics: Classification Tasks: The framework not only computes the overall accuracy in real-time but also automatically generates a comprehensive confusion matrix, aiding in the intuitive diagnosis of class imbalances or misclassification biases. Regression Tasks: Within the original physical dimensional space (following automatic inverse normalization), the framework strictly calculates the mean squared error, mean absolute error, and the coefficient of determination. The coefficient of determination provides a more precise measurement of the goodness of fit in high-fidelity trajectory tracking tasks. Debugging Techniques: Mandatory Gradient Clipping: To fundamentally circumvent the gradient explosion problem inherent to traditional RNN/LSTM architectures during long-sequence training, the underlying function mandates gradient clipping prior to every optimizer step. Serving as an intrinsic protective safeguard of the framework, this significantly enhances the training robustness of the model on datasets with low signal-to-noise ratios. Dynamic Early Stopping & Best Checkpoint: To effectively combat model overfitting, the framework deeply integrates a dynamic early stopping mechanism tied to validation set performance during the training process. The model automatically monitors and saves the weight state that achieves optimal validation performance. If no improvement is observed within the tolerance period, training is terminated prematurely, thereby preventing overfitting. Learning Rate Adjustment: An excessively large learning rate can lead to training instability, while one that is too small may result in unacceptably slow training. The training process can be optimized by progressively decaying the learning rate or by employing adaptive learning rate optimizers.

The design of this LSTM implementation framework exhibits exceptional scalability, enabling it to flexibly address core pain points across diverse domains: Motor Intention Decoding (Motor Imagery, MI): Motor imagery tasks require subjects to mentally simulate specific actions without generating muscular electromyographic activity. During this period, scalp electroencephalography (EEG) exhibits non-stationary Event-Related Desynchronization/Synchronization (ERD/ERS) phenomena. The LSTM can sensitively capture such faint, temporally diffuse dynamic phase-transition features, thereby achieving highly accurate intention classification and providing a reliable control source for neural prosthetics and rehabilitation exoskeletons. Emotional State Recognition: Neurophysiological signals provide the most authentic underlying emotional mapping, stripped of subjective cognitive disguise. By extracting multi-band feature sequences, bidirectional LSTMs can precisely track the dynamic temporal evolution of human emotions across the time dimension, subsequently outputting high-resolution emotional state evaluations. Silent Speech Interfaces: For patients who have lost their vocal articulation capabilities, the decoding of "inner speech" based on brain-computer interfaces has emerged as a cutting-edge challenge. When patients silently rehearse command vocabularies in their minds, cortical networks generate neural spike sequences with robust temporal architectures. This represents the optimal theoretical focal point for LSTMs to demonstrate their capabilities in long-term semantic memory and temporal dependency mining. Potential for Ultra-low Latency Online Closed-loop Control: In the clinical deployment of BCI, computational latency is the critical lifeline determining the success rate of prosthetic control or cursor clicks. When configured as unidirectional (by passing --no_bidirectional), the LSTM architecture employs strict causal time-step progression. This eliminates the need to wait for future neural data windows during inference, thereby offering ultra-low latency characteristics that are ideal for seamless deployment in real-time closed-loop systems. Conversely, for offline high-precision decoding tasks, the default bidirectional mode can be maintained to capture the complete global context.

The Language LSTM is an end-to-end deep architecture specifically designed for Neural Speech Decoding and the analysis of high-frequency temporal physiological signals. In the field of Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI), particularly under the "Silent Speech" paradigm, there often exists extreme Temporal Misalignment and highly non-linear long-range dependencies between the generation rhythm of neural spike sequences and the final output of linguistic symbols. Centered on the 11_Speech-style Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) theory, this architecture thoroughly breaks the reliance of traditional decoders on forced frame-level alignment. By combining the global temporal modeling capabilities of deep recurrent neural networks with the asynchronous marginalization inference of CTC, it achieves an autonomous mapping from highly noisy, high-dimensional neural dynamic manifolds to discrete semantic symbols. From the perspectives of theoretical mechanisms and network topology, this architecture consists of the following three core modules: Frontend Filtration & Manifold Unfolding Neural population activities are often accompanied by high-frequency transient Poisson noise. Instead of simply passing the signal through, the underlying SpeechFeatureFrontend of the model executes a rigorous set of physical preprocessing steps: High-frequency Noise Filtration: Utilizes the GaussianSmoothing module to perform one-dimensional convolutional smoothing on the input channels, filtering out high-frequency artifacts in the time domain while preserving the low-frequency population firing envelope. Cross-day Domain Shift Compensation: To address the physical displacement of microelectrode arrays over time, the model has built-in day_weights and day_bias parameter matrices. It performs linear calibration of the cross-day feature space directly at the input end via affine transformations. Receptive Field Reconstruction: Following Softsign activation, the nn.Unfold mechanism is employed to segment the one-dimensional temporal signal into overlapping two-dimensional windowed features. This operation significantly enriches the local dynamic context observable by subsequent networks at each time step. Deep Bi-directional Contextual Capture The inference core of the model relies on a multi-layer Bi-directional LSTM (Bi-LSTM) network. Unlike unidirectional evolution, the bi-directional architecture, when processing a complete single speech trial, can simultaneously converge historical firing memory and future evolutionary trends at the current time step. Coupled with the compact handling of variable-length sequences using pack_padded_sequence, the model can extract deep semantic trajectories hidden within lengthy neural impulses with extreme precision. Asynchronous Sequence Decoding & CTC Loss When the human brain formulates continuous speech, the length of neural firing is never equal to the length of the actual output characters. At the output end, the model maps features to the vocabulary space through cascaded LayerNorm and Dropout, and subsequently introduces nn.CTCLoss. By introducing a Blank Token and performing probability marginalization across all possible alignment paths, this mechanism endows the model with the robust capability to learn sequence mappings end-to-end without relying on any frame-by-frame labels.

The training stability of the Language LSTM is exceptionally dependent on the purity of the underlying data stream. The framework establishes exceedingly rigorous engineering specifications for the ingestion of temporal neural tensors: Rigorous Neural Temporal Tensorization: The underlying engine strictly mandates that the feature matrix X must be a standard three-dimensional tensor (N, T, C) (i.e., trials, time steps, channels), while the semantic label y must be a two-dimensional sequence (N, S). Dynamic Boundary Alignment: Given the variable lengths of speech intentions, the framework requires that all samples be accompanied by true physical boundary records (X_len and y_len). When calculating CTC paths and performing padding operations, the network strictly adheres to these boundaries for dynamic truncation, completely eliminating the contamination of gradients by invalid padded data. Unidirectional Statistic Freezing: To counteract the vast dimensional discrepancies between different channels, the system adopts an absolutely isolated anti-leakage strategy when fitting the feature distribution (Z-score statistics). The mean and standard deviation are independently fitted solely within the training domain and mapped unidirectionally to the validation and test domains in a frozen state, ensuring the absolute authenticity of generalization evaluations. Stochastic Perturbation Injection (in Low Signal-to-Noise Ratio Environments): During training forward propagation, the framework supports the dynamic injection of Gaussian white noise (white_noise_sd) and channel-level constant offset noise (constant_offset_sd). This enhancement strategy, grounded in physical reality, forces the network to learn invariant feature representations that are more robust against electrode impedance variations and environmental electromagnetic interference.

To guarantee the precision of academic reproducibility, the core override rules (Overrides) for two distinct distribution shift scenarios—single-day decoding and cross-day decoding—have been exhaustively integrated.

In the domain of continuous language decoding, model evaluation must transcend simple frame-level accuracy and focus on the fidelity of the global sequence topology: Levenshtein Sequence Evaluation: The framework discards the rigid cross-entropy metric, adopting the Character Error Rate (CER) as the sole guiding north star. By calculating the minimum number of insertions, deletions, and substitutions (i.e., Levenshtein distance) between the model's greedily decoded sequence and the true semantic symbols, CER can most intuitively reflect the usability of the BCI system in real-world communication scenarios. Mandatory Dynamical Stabilization: When processing ultra-long neural spike sequences composed of hundreds or thousands of time steps, Bi-LSTMs are highly susceptible to arithmetic overflow caused by gradient accumulation. To thoroughly circumvent Gradient Explosion, the underlying optimization engine is hard-coded to execute strict clip_grad_norm_ (threshold 1.0) clipping between every forward/backward step, serving as a physical safety perimeter for model training. Adaptive Annealing & Generalization Defense: The system deeply integrates the ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler. When the model is trapped in a local saddle point on the error rate surface, the scheduler automatically performs learning rate halving annealing. Coupled with rigorous validation set state monitoring and the optimal model Checkpoint rollback mechanism, it perfectly intercepts the collapse of generalization capability caused by overfitting the training domain during the later stages of training.

The meticulous refinement of the Language LSTM architecture and its internal mechanisms directly addresses several ultimate challenges in modern closed-loop speech brain-computer interfaces: Breaking the Constraints of Strict Temporal Alignment: Traditional models based on Hidden Markov Models (HMM) or pure Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLP) require training data to possess exceptionally precise "frame-to-phoneme" level labels. In contrast, the Language LSTM leverages the marginalization capability of CTC, allowing researchers to directly utilize full-sentence text as target labels for training. This characteristic makes it possible to "directly generate natural language sequences from brain intentions," vastly reducing the manual labor cost of neural data annotation. Feature Extraction of Micro-Neural Dynamics: Raw, high-sampling-rate electrophysiological signals are replete with microscopic redundancies. Relying on the original SpeechFeatureFrontend mechanism, the model performs not merely noise reduction, but projects the time series into a higher-dimensional feature subspace via window unfolding (Unfold). This expansion of the local receptive field grants the LSTM extremely high sensitivity and fidelity when extracting weak cortical pronunciation commands. Combating Non-stationary Domain Shifts: The greatest nightmare facing implantable electrodes or scalp EEG systems is the drift of signal distributions over time. The parameter set provided by this model allows for flexible switching of alignment strategies between single-day and cross-day scenarios. In particular, disabling day_idx coupled with strong regularization provides researchers with a standardized and highly compelling testing tool to verify whether the features extracted by the model itself are merely "overfitted memories" or genuine "neural semantic invariants."

This project provides a preprocessing method for the THINGS-EEG dataset, transforming raw EEG signals into model-ready data. The dataset contains high-temporal-resolution EEG responses from 10 participants to naturalistic object images, recorded using a Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) paradigm. Each participant completed 82,160 trials, covering 16,740 image conditions. The training set consists of 16,540 (image categories) × 10 (images per category) × 4 (repetitions), and the test set contains 200 (image categories) × 1 (image) × 80 (repetitions). Training and test stimuli were presented in a pseudo-random order, and target images were used to reduce blinking and other artifacts. EEG was recorded with a 64-channel EASYCAP (electrodes arranged according to the standard 10–10 system) and a BrainVision actiCHamp amplifier at 1000 Hz sampling rate, with online filtering (0.1–100 Hz) and referencing to the Fz electrode.

Acquisition Hardware: A 64-channel EASYCAP EEG cap (electrode layout following the international 10-10 system) paired with a Brainvision actiCHamp amplifier. Online Processing: During data acquisition, a real-time 0.1 Hz to 100 Hz band-pass filter is applied online. Reference Electrode: The Fz electrode is used as the online reference.

Core Paradigm: Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) paradigm. Stimulus Content: Object images presented against natural backgrounds. Quality Control Mechanism: “Target images” are interspersed within the image sequence to control participant attention and reduce the impact of blinking and other motion artifacts on the EEG signals. Experiment Scale: Each participant performs 82,160 trials (the training set includes 16,540 image categories, and the test set includes 200 image categories).

Raw EEG data from 10 participants. The raw EEG data in each .zip file is organized into four recording phases and training/test partitions according to the following directory structure: /sub-01 │ └───ses-01 │ └───raw_eeg_test.npy │ └───raw_eeg_training.npy │ ... │ └───ses-04 └───raw_eeg_test.npy └───raw_eeg_training.npy

This pipeline is designed to convert raw EEG data and corresponding stimulus image data into a standardized tensor format suitable for multimodal model training, covering the complete workflow from EEG signal preprocessing, image feature extraction, to feature and label recombination and storage. 1. Signal Loading & Epoching Using the core function epoching(), raw EEG data is converted to MNE format and subjected to channel selection. Continuous data is strictly segmented into time windows (epochs) from 0 to 1000 milliseconds following stimulus onset, with baseline correction using the data from 200 milliseconds before the stimulus. All retained electrode signals are downsampled to 250 Hz and categorized and sorted based on the image condition that evoked the EEG signal. 2. Signal Denoising & Whitening The mvnn() function is used to apply Multivariate Noise Normalization (MVNN) to both the training and testing sets. By calculating the covariance matrix of the EEG data and taking the square root of its inverse matrix (whitening matrix), the signal is transformed to eliminate inter-channel correlations. Additionally, for all EEG trials corresponding to repeatedly presented stimuli, averaging is performed to effectively improve the signal-to-noise ratio of the EEG image. 3. Image Preprocessing & Feature Extraction Stimulus images that induce emotion are spatially adjusted, uniformly scaled to a 224×224 pixel size, and normalized. The images are then input into the pre-trained CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) model’s image encoder to extract high-dimensional image visual feature maps. 4. Data Integration & Storage After processing with multiple scripts (processing.py, obtain_feature_maps_clip.py, processed.py), the preprocessed EEG data and image feature data are saved by participant (e.g., sub01), divided into training and testing sets, in .npz format. Upon loading, the EEG feature tensor with shape (number of samples, number of channels, number of time points), the image feature tensor with shape (number of samples, features), and the strictly aligned image category label tensor with shape (number of samples, labels) are readily available.

Stabilization_MLP is a pipeline model specifically designed to address the non-stationarity of neural signals across days or different time periods in Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI). In implanted microelectrode array recordings, the distribution of neural population signals drifts over time. This model hypothesizes that, despite the drift in neural firing at the observational level, the underlying low-dimensional neural dynamical manifold remains relatively stable. From the perspective of underlying theory and engineering implementation, the core architecture of this model consists of the following five key components: Gaussian Factor Analysis Base Stabilizer During the training phase, the model extracts a specific number of trials at the end of the training data and uses the Expectation-Maximization algorithm to fit a standard Gaussian Factor Analysis model with diagonal private variances. Serving as the base stabilizer, this module is responsible for extracting pure initial latent variables from high-dimensional and noisy neural channels. Dynamic Cross-Day Calibration When faced with drifted data on a brand-new test day, the model refuses to retrain from scratch. Instead, it utilizes only a very small number of calibration trials at the very beginning of the test set (controlled by system-preset proportions or thresholds) to calculate and update the stabilizer parameters for that day. This few-shot calibration mechanism significantly reduces the daily maintenance cost of BCI systems. Orthogonal Alignment of Loading Matrices To eliminate the rotational uncertainty inherent in factor analysis across different days, the model automatically identifies stable loading rows by comparing the base loading matrix with the updated loading matrix, based on preset tolerance thresholds and channel counts. Subsequently, the system solves for the optimal orthogonal transformation matrix (i.e., the Procrustes alignment algorithm), forcibly and seamlessly rotating and mapping the latent variables of the test day back to the base training space. Task-Decoupled MLP Decoding After acquiring aligned and highly standardized latent features, the model connects to a downstream Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) for intention decoding. Tailored to different paradigms, the model enforces strict code decoupling at the underlying level: Discrete Intention Classification: Invokes a standard classification network to delineate non-linear decision boundaries. Continuous Kinematic Regression: To achieve high-fidelity trajectory tracking, the regression task specifically refactors the regression network, employing the Scaled Conjugate Gradient method for highly efficient weight optimization. Target-Driven Fine-Tuning Particularly in continuous regression tasks, to further squeeze decoding performance, the model natively supports weight inheritance and fine-tuning mechanisms. Upon completing orthogonal alignment, the model concatenates the original training data with the current calibration data and performs secondary optimization on the decoder's weights for a specified number of epochs, achieving final compensation for minor non-linear shifts.

The Stabilization_MLP architecture maintains highly stringent engineering control over input features to ensure the absolute safety of matrix operations and the effectiveness of alignment: Trial Pooling & Flattening: Since traditional factor analysis lacks temporal memory capabilities, the model incorporates a built-in feature pooling method. This forcibly flattens the three-dimensional temporal tensor—containing trials, time steps, and channels—along the time axis, converting it into a two-dimensional matrix to feed the Expectation-Maximization algorithm for covariance estimation. Strict Statistical Isolation: The aligned latent features must be standardized before being fed into the decoder. The model adheres to strict data isolation: it calculates the mean and standard deviation solely using the training set's latent variables, freezes these statistics, and unidirectionally applies them to the feature scaling of the test set, completely eradicating cross-day data leakage. Chronological Calibration Split: In real-world BCI scenarios, calibration data can only be drawn from the beginning of the test day. Through slicing logic, the model absolutely guarantees that the samples used to update the stabilizer chronologically precede the validation samples used for performance evaluation.

To maintain absolute data integrity, all specific baselines and override rules for cross-day decoding, single-day decoding, and Classification/Regression tasks have been fully integrated.

For the performance evaluation of non-stationary systems, the model features built-in, highly automated diagnostic metrics and visualization outputs: Multi-dimensional Regression Metrics: In continuous kinematic (e.g., spatial arm coordinates) reconstruction, the model not only outputs the globally averaged coefficient of determination but also forcibly decouples and outputs the goodness of fit for each independent dimension. This greatly assists in troubleshooting the physical causes of decoding failures in specific directions within the control system. Optimization Loss Curve: During simulation training, the system automatically captures and plots the loss curve, allowing researchers to intuitively determine whether oscillations or premature convergence occur during the conjugate gradient descent process. Chronological Robustness Plot: After evaluating multiple test days, the system sorts them in natural chronological order and outputs a cross-day trend chart of accuracy or the coefficient of determination, complete with a mean baseline. This visually demonstrates the anti-degradation ability of the manifold alignment algorithm against the passage of time.

The theoretical mechanisms of Stabilization_MLP directly address the core pain points of long-term implanted BCIs, demonstrating exceptionally high scientific research and clinical application value: Stabilizing BCIs against Signal Drift Over weeks or months of recording, micromotion between the microelectrodes and brain tissue occurs, causing changes in the neurons recorded by the same channel. By extracting the intrinsic manifold and utilizing few-shot learning for orthogonal rotation alignment, the model effectively compensates for this deviation. This deceives the downstream decoder into perceiving the input data as still being in its initial state, thereby maintaining high-level, long-term stable decoding. Ultra-low Cost Daily Calibration Traditional BCIs require patients to spend significant amounts of time every day performing repetitive actions to retrain the entire decoding model. Relying on the preset mechanisms of this architecture, the system only requires an extremely small amount of data for baseline warm-up at startup each day. By updating the relatively small loading matrix, it can instantly revert to a usable state, vastly improving the user experience for clinical patients. Calibration-driven Online Real-time Decoding This manifold stabilization architecture achieves an exceptionally elegant engineering separation at the boundary between offline and online operations. Before initiating the daily system, it requires only a minimal amount of time during the offline or preparatory phase to extract few-shot data for updating and aligning the stabilization matrix. Once the orthogonal rotation matrix is locked in, the downstream decoupled network can unleash ultimate inference speeds. This endows the model with remarkably strong online real-time closed-loop control capabilities, ensuring that clinical patients can enjoy ultra-low latency prosthetic manipulation experiences.

This model provides a Transformer sequence decoding architecture specifically designed for neurophysiological signals (such as Spikes, LFPs, etc.). Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) data typically exhibits complex temporal dependencies and cross-channel correlations. Traditional RNN models are prone to the vanishing gradient problem when processing long sequences. This model breaks through this limitation. Its core hypothesis is that utilizing the Self-Attention mechanism can directly establish global dependencies between arbitrary time steps, thereby more efficiently capturing the dynamic representations of neural population activities. From the perspectives of code implementation and network topology, this Transformer architecture consists of the following core modules: Input Projection The raw input typically consists of neural signals with high-dimensional feature channels. The model first maps the feature dimensions to a unified embedding dimension via a linear projection layer, ensuring feature space consistency for subsequent attention computations. Positional Encoding For tasks with strong temporal dependencies, such as continuous kinematic trajectory decoding, the model introduces fixed-period Sine/Cosine Positional Encoding. This mechanism injects absolute temporal information into the inherently non-directional self-attention mechanism, enabling the model to perceive the chronological sequence of the spike trains. Multi-Head Attention Encoder The core backbone network is constructed by stacking multiple Transformer encoder layers. Through the multi-head mechanism, the model computes the scaled dot-product attention of Queries, Keys, and Values in parallel across different subspaces. This mechanism completely shatters the constraints of temporal distance, enabling the model to directly establish neural action potential dependencies spanning hundreds of milliseconds with a global receptive field. Dual-Track Task Head To accommodate generalized BCI paradigms, the model incorporates highly flexible decoding logic at the output end: Pooled Decoding: For discrete intention recognition or entire-signal classification, the model performs average pooling across the temporal dimension, subsequently connecting to a linear classification head. Seq-Target Decoding: When sequence target prediction is enabled, the model bypasses the pooling layer and directly applies a linear projection to the hidden states at each time step. This outputs a feature tensor containing the temporal dimension, precisely adapting to the real-time regression prediction of continuous kinematic coordinates (e.g., 2D spatial coordinates).

To ensure the robustness of the BCIJelly platform when processing invasive datasets from diverse sources and paradigms, the model's underlying data pipeline incorporates strict tensorization standards and preprocessing logic: Multi-key Tensorization The model natively supports high-dimensional data parsing. The data stream is automatically backward-compatible with various feature keys and label keys commonly used in the neuroscience community, and strictly validates that the input tensors are in a standard three-dimensional format (number of samples, time steps, and feature channels). Adaptive Label Encoding For heterogeneous labels common in classification tasks (such as One-hot encoding, strings, or floating-point indices), the system features built-in automated cleaning and parsing functions. This mechanism can automatically clean garbled text, parse byte strings, and map multi-class labels into dense integer IDs starting from 0, ensuring seamless integration with the cross-entropy loss function. Statistical Normalization For continuous regression tasks, the model not only requires strict dimensional alignment of the target tensors but also mandates statistical fitting prior to training. The system automatically calculates the mean and standard deviation of both features and labels within the training set distribution. It then applies rigorous Z-score standardization and inverse standardization transformations during the validation and testing phases, thereby ensuring the stability of gradient descent.

Original input channels are mapped to this unified dimension via linear projection. Note: For the Jango_force cross-day classification task, doubling to 128 is recommended. n_heads int 4 Number of heads in the multi-head attention mechanism. The embedding dimensionality must be strictly divisible by this value. Note: For the Jango_force cross-day classification task, an increase to 8 is recommended. dropout float 0.1 Neuron dropout probability within attention weights and feed-forward networks, utilized for noise suppression. [Core Strategy]: Must be elevated to 0.3 for cross-day decoding scenarios; set to 0.2 for the Jango_force dataset. weight_decay float 0.0 L2 regularization weight for the optimizer. [Core Strategy]: Set to 0 for single-day tasks. For cross-day tasks, a penalty term must be introduced, recommending an override to 0.001 (set to 0.0001 for the Jango_force dataset). seq_target bool False Sequence target toggle. When enabled (True), executes step-wise frame-level prediction; when disabled (False), outputs a single prediction post-pooling.

Parameter / CLI Flag Type Default Detailed Description output_dir str "./outputs" Root storage directory for logs, metric curves, and model weight files. --save_ckpt / --no_save_ckpt flag True Controls whether to save the model weight files demonstrating optimal performance on the validation set. --plot flag False Boolean flag indicating whether to automatically invoke the plotting module to generate metric curves upon training completion. --task str None Explicitly specifies the current log task type to assist in rendering the corresponding metric curves.

To accurately measure the model's performance in neural decoding tasks, the system features a built-in dual-track optimization strategy and a comprehensive evaluation matrix: Task-Isolated Optimization For classification tasks, the model employs a standard Adam optimizer paired with Cross-Entropy Loss. Conversely, for complex continuous regression tasks, the model switches to the more robust AdamW optimizer, mandating the integration of a Cosine Annealing learning rate scheduler and Smooth L1 Loss. This ensures convergence smoothness while tolerating extreme outliers in neural signals. Multi-Dimensional Metrics Classification: Each validation step not only outputs accuracy and cross-entropy loss but also comprehensively records the confusion matrix, facilitating the tracking of biases caused by class imbalance. Regression: Discarding the reliance on a single Mean Squared Error metric, the model synchronously calculates the Mean Squared Error, Mean Absolute Error, and the Coefficient of Determination. The Coefficient of Determination provides an intuitive reflection of the proportion of neural dynamic variance explained by the model. Automated Logging & Visualization The system tracks training and validation metrics as well as learning rate changes in real-time for each epoch. The platform can generate dual-Y-axis training trend curves (supporting side-by-side comparisons of loss descent and metric ascent) and optimal metric bar charts for batch tasks with a single click.

Based on engineering practices, this Transformer architecture has been designed to adeptly handle classic invasive and non-invasive BCI datasets at the level of top-tier conferences and journals, demonstrating exceptional domain transferability: Kinematic Regression Targeted at non-human primate BCI datasets. By enabling sequence target prediction and strictly aligning the spatial coordinate dimensions, the model can accurately read cortical Spike signals to reconstruct the continuous kinematic coordinates of monkeys performing tasks frame by frame. Discrete State Classification For datasets involving fine finger movement recognition, music, or emotional state classification, the model utilizes the global pooling mechanism to perform dimensionality reduction and feature fusion on entire high-dimensional inputs, effectively outputting multi-class intention classification results. Visual Coding When processing probe data, the attention distribution mechanism of the Transformer allows researchers to reverse-track long-term sequence data, uncovering non-linear dependency features between stimulus signals and the firing activities of neuronal populations. Offline Global Analysis & Block-Online Decoding Unlike the frame-by-frame progression of RNNs, the Transformer's reliance on global context windows endows it with unparalleled long-term feature mining capabilities. This gives it a dominant advantage in Offline neural decoding, sleep staging analysis, or cognitive state reconstruction. For BCI control tasks requiring real-time responses, the Transformer is better suited for a Block-Online paradigm—introducing an extremely brief computational latency (e.g., accumulating short data blocks) in exchange for exceptionally high global resolution of spatial and temporal neural correlations.
